item.title: Getting Started with Docker: A Beginner’s Guide
item.author: Pravesh Sudha
item.content:encoded: Welcome to the world of DevOps! Today, we’re diving into an exciting tool that solves the classic software engineering problem:“It worked on my machine, but not on yours!”Yep, we’ve all been there. But thanks to , this headache is now a thing of the past!In this blog, we’ll explore how Docker has revolutionized the DevOps industry, saving companies in computing costs. We’ll also get hands-on by creating a for a cloud-native monitoring application that tracks .So, grab your coffee, and let’s get started! 🚀💡 What is Docker? Understanding Containers & Why They MatterDocker is a platform that allows us to develop, ship, and run applications in containers.Wait… containers? Shipping? What’s going on here? 🚢Let’s slow down and break it down step by step.Think of Docker as a tool that helps us create a of our application. Using this blueprint, we can run the application inside a .But what exactly is a in Docker? 🤔A is like a lightweight operating system that includes everything needed to run an application — its dependencies, libraries, and configurations.Here’s the of using Docker:We create a (this is the exact name it should have).Inside the , we define how our application should be built and run.Using some , we generate a — this is the actual of our application.Finally, we use the to run a , where our application will actually execute.Why Use Docker? Isn’t This More Complicated?At first, Docker might seem complex, but here’s why it’s :🔹 :Before Docker, we used to run applications on the cloud. However, VMs often , leading to . With Docker, we use that run on VMs, making applications much faster and more efficient.🔹 Consistency Across Environments:When we create a , we choose a for our project (like Ubuntu, Alpine, or Python). This ensures that all developers and servers use the exact same environment, preventing the “works on my machine but not yours”issue.🔹 Multiple Applications on One Machine:With Docker, we can run multiple applications on the same VM. Before Docker, we could usually run only one application per VM. This means better and .Now that we understand , let’s move forward and build something cool with it! 🚀💡 Installing Docker & Running Your First ContainerEnough theory — let’s jump into action! 🏃💨Step 1: Installing DockerFirst, we need to install Docker on our system. Here’s how:✅ : Download from Docker’s official website. It provides an easy-to-use GUI along with the Docker CLI.✅ : You can install Docker using terminal commands. If you’re on , run:sudo apt update -ysudo apt install docker.io -ysudo usermod -aG docker $USER && newgrp dockerdocker --version # This will display the installed Docker version.Once installed, Docker is ready to roll! 🎉Step 2: Understanding DockerHub & Image ManagementEarlier, we talked about 📝 helping us create 📦. But what if we the image we created? Our would be gone!To avoid this, we have — a cloud-based repository where we can store and share Docker images, just like .: Open for everyone to use.: Only accessible to authorized users.This allows teams to collaborate and deploy applications consistently across different environments.Step 3: Running Your First Docker ContainerLet’s test Docker by running a :docker run -d -p 8080:80 docker/welcome-to-dockerdocker run → Runs a new container.-d → Runs the container in (in the background).-p 8080:80 → Maps on your local machine to inside the container.docker/welcome-to-docker → The we are running, which is pre-configured to display a welcome message.Now, open your web browser and go to:You should see a Docker Congratulations page! 🎉This page is served by the image, which contains a simple web server (NGINX or Apache)displaying the welcome message.Now that you’ve run your first Docker container, let’s move on to 🚀💡 Building Our Own Docker Image: Cloud Monitoring AppNow that we’ve experimented with a demo Docker container, it’s time to build ! 🚀We’ll use a Flask-based cloud monitoring application that displays of the machine it runs on.Step 1: Cloning the Project RepositoryFirst, let’s clone the project from GitHub:git clone https://github.com/Pravesh-Sudha/cloud-native-monitoring-app.gitNext, navigate inside the project directory:cd cloud-native-monitoring-appInside this directory, you’ll find a . Let’s break it down!Step 2: Understanding the DockerfileHere’s the for our Flask-based cloud monitoring app:# 1️⃣ Set the base imageFROM python:3.9-slim-buster # 2️⃣ Set the working directory inside the containerWORKDIR /app # 3️⃣ Copy the dependency file into the containerCOPY requirements.txt . # 4️⃣ Install dependenciesRUN pip3 install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt # 5️⃣ Copy the entire project into the containerCOPY . . # 6️⃣ Set environment variablesENV FLASK_RUN_HOST=0.0.0.0 # 7️⃣ Expose the application’s portEXPOSE 5000 # 8️⃣ Define the command to run the Flask appCMD ["flask", "run"]📌 FROM python:3.9-slim-buster → This sets our .We’re using python:3.9-slim-buster, a version of Python 3.9.Slim images are in size, making the container more efficient.📌 WORKDIR /app → This sets the inside the container.Just like we create a folder for our project, we define /app as the directory where our application code will live inside the container.📌 COPY requirements.txt . → This copies requirements.txt into the container.This file contains all the required for our Flask app.📌 RUN pip3 install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt → This installs the required .📌 COPY . . → This copies our application files into the container.📌 ENV FLASK_RUN_HOST=0.0.0.0 → This sets .Here, we define FLASK_RUN_HOST=0.0.0.0 to allow our app to run properly inside a container or VM.📌 EXPOSE 5000 → This exposes , the default port for Flask applications.📌 CMD ["flask", "run"] → This defines the that will start our Flask app.Step 3: Building the Docker ImageNow, let’s create a from this project. Run the following command:docker build -t my-app:v1 .🔹 -t my-app:v1 → Tags the image as with version v1.🔹 . → Tells Docker to use the as the build context.Step 4: Running the Docker ContainerOnce the image is built successfully, let’s run it as a :docker run -d -p 5000:5000 my-app:v1🔹 -d → Runs the container in (in the background).🔹 -p 5000:5000 → Maps on your local machine to inside the container.Now, open your browser and go to:🎉 You should see the application running!Each time you refresh the page, the CPU and memory usage metrics will .Now that we’ve successfully built and run our Dockerized cloud monitoring app.Congratulations! 🎉 You’ve just taken your first steps into the world of Docker by learning how it solves real-world software deployment problems, running a demo container, and even Dockerizing a Flask-based cloud monitoring application.Here’s a quick recap of what we covered:✅ A platform for building, shipping, and running applications in lightweight containers.✅ It solves compatibility issues, improves resource utilization, and allows multiple applications to run efficiently on the same machine.✅ We installed Docker, ran a sample container, created a , built a , and launched a for our project.This is just the beginning! Docker is a widely used in DevOps, CI/CD pipelines, and cloud deployments. The next step? Try experimenting with , push your image to , or deploy your containerized app to ! 🚀If you found this blog helpful, feel free to share it with your fellow DevOps enthusiasts! Have questions? Drop them in the comments — I’d love to help. 😊

item.title: Freeipa/RedHat IdM API
item.author: Aditya Jambhalikar
item.content:encoded: Red Hat Identity Management (IdM) provides a centralised and unified way to manage identity stores, authentication, policies, and authorisation policies in a Linux-based domain.It also provides an option to use API (IPA API) to perform all the tasks programatically.There are lots of ways to use it, but let’s check 3 popular ways which are curl , python and ansibleWhen it comes to quick use of API, we always think of using cURL. To use it for freeipa api, we first need to obtain cookies (create session) by logging in and then use the cookies for any further operationThe first thing to do is to define the environmental variables of the username, password, and cookies file.export COOKIEJAR="/tmp/ipa" # path to store the cookie fileexport _USERNAME="admin" # or any other user with appropriate permission for the task you want to perform export _PASSWORD="<admin_password>" # Notice the whitespace before export, # It will make sure that the entry is not added to the termianl command history The above step is optional, you can otherwise just pass the values directly in the request.The next step is to login and obtain the cookiescurl -k \-H Referer:https://<ipa_fqdn>/ipa \-H "Content-Type:application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \-H "Accept:text/plain" \-c $COOKIEJAR -b $COOKIEJAR \--data "user=$_USERNAME&password=$_PASSWORD" \https://<ipa_fqdn>/ipa/session/login_passwordIf this is successful, then you can send any requeste.g. Let’s fetch all the users present in freeipacurl -k \ -H Referer:https://<ipa_fqdn>/ipa \ -H "Content-Type:application/json" \ -H "Accept:application/json"\ -c $COOKIEJAR -b $COOKIEJAR \ -d '{"method": "user_find", "params": [[], {"all": true, "raw": true}]}' \ https://<ipa_fqdn>/ipa/session/jsonThe only part that will change in any request is the data-dThe Python way is similar as well. We create a session and then use it for any operation. There could be many ways to do it. But I prefer defining a class and using the session object.import requestsclass IPA: def __init__(self, ipa_url): self.ipa_url = ipa_url self.session_url = f"https://{self.ipa_url}/ipa/session/json" self.referer = f"https://{self.ipa_url}/ipa" self.session = requests.Session() self.req_header = {"Referer": self.referer, 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 'Accept': 'application/json'} def login(self, username="admin", password="default_admin_password"): data = {"user": username, "password": password} login_url = f"https://{self.ipa_url}/ipa/session/login_password" header = {"Referer": self.referer, "Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded", "Accept": "text/plain"} res = self.session.post(login_url, headers=header, data=data, verify=False) def ipa_request(self, data): res = self.session.post(self.session_url, headers=self.req_header, data=json.dumps(data), verify=False) data = {"method": "user_find", "params": [[], {"all": True, "sizelimit": 0}]} res = self.ipa_request(data) return res def group_add_member(self, group_cn, users): data = {"method": "group_add_member", "params": [[f"{group_cn}"], {"all": True, "user": users}]} print(f"\nrequest to send is:\n{data}") res = self.ipa_request(data)if __name__ == "__main__": ipa = IPA(ipa_url="ipa.local.com") users = ipa.user_find() all_users = users["result"]["result"] print(all_users) res = ipa.group_add_member("test_group", ["user1", "user2"])The above code will print all users present in freeipa and add the users user1 and user2 to the user group test_groupCan you modify the code and add the function to create the users and user groups?Ansible is the best when it comes to configuring and managing different kinds of environments. The community general provides a module to configure the freeipa objects. It doesn’t support everything, it has most of the useful configuration objects# vim: ft=yaml---- name: Freeipa configuration hosts: localhost environment: IPA_PASS: "<admin_password>" # This can be env var or vault var IPA_HOST: ipa.local.com - "/root/hostgroup_list.yaml" - name: Add hostgroups community.general.ipa_hostgroup: name: "{{ item.name }}" description: "{{ item.description }}" validate_certs: False with_items: "{{ hostgroup_list }}" tags: hostgroups/root/hostgroup_list.yamlhostgroup_list: - description: 'test_group'After this, you can run the playbookansible-playbook /root/ipa_playbook.yamlThis was just a simple example, but there are many better ways to do ite.g. creating an inventory files, group_vars, encrypting IPA_PASS with ansible-vault etcThe freeipa API makes things easier and reduces operational overheadFreeipa/RedHat IdM API was originally published in DevOps.dev on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

item.title: gRPC: For High-Performance Microservices Communication
item.author: DAMAN
item.content:encoded: gRPC (Google Remote Procedure Call) is a high-performance, open-source RPC framework developed by Google. It enables efficient communication between services in microservices architectures by using Protocol Buffers (protobufs) for serialization and HTTP/2 as the transport protocol. Here’s a detailed breakdown of gRPC:gRPC is a modern, high-performance framework for inter-service communication, supporting multiple programming languages. It is commonly used in to enable fast and efficient communication between backend services.As applications grow in complexity — especially in microservices architectures — efficient communication between services becomes critical. Traditional REST-based APIs, while widely used, have performance and scalability limitations. gRPC solves these issues by offering faster, more efficient, and scalable communicationUses HTTP/2 Instead of HTTP/1.1 Multiple requests can be sent in parallel over a single connection, reducing overhead. Supports , where both client and server can send messages independently. Reduces network bandwidth consumption.Protocol Buffers (Protobuf) Instead of JSON Binary serialization is more compact and faster than JSON. Prevents data structure mismatches between client and server.Backward and forward compatibility: Older clients can communicate with newer servers and vice versa.Supports Multiple LanguagesOfficial support for Go, Python, Java, Node.js, C++, C#, Ruby, PHP, etc.Makes it easier to build (multi-language) microservices.Authentication and SecuritySupports (Transport Layer Security) for encryption.Can integrate with OAuth, JWT, and API Gateway security models.You define and using Protocol Buffers.Generate Code for Client & ServerThe .proto file is compiled using the protoc compiler to generate for the target language.The server implements the defined RPC methods.The client uses the generated stub to make remote procedure calls.Unary RPC (Simple Request-Response)Client sends a request → Server processes it → Server sends back a response.Client sends a request → Server streams multiple responses back.Client sends multiple requests → Server processes and sends a single response.Bidirectional Streaming RPCBoth client and server send and receive multiple messages asynchronously.✅ Microservices communication✅ High-performance APIs with low latency✅ Streaming data (video, chat, real-time updates)✅ Language-agnostic distributed systems✅ IoT and mobile apps (efficient data transfer)❌ Many browsers don’t support gRPC natively (use REST or GraphQL instead).❌ Client-side apps that require direct browser support: gRPC-Web is needed for browser support.Best Practices for gRPC in Kubernetes: Prefer Envoy or Nginx over default Kubernetes proxiesImplement Client-Side Load Balancing: Distribute connection logicLeverage Service Mesh Technologies: Istio, Linkerd: Continuous performance evaluationConsider Alternative Deployment Strategies: StatefulSets, headless servicesAs cloud-native architectures continue to evolve, gRPC is positioning itself as a critical technology for building interconnected, efficient, and flexible distributed systems. With growing support from major tech companies and an active open-source community, gRPC is more than just a communication protocol — it’s a strategic approach to designing robust, performant microservices. In the world of distributed computing, gRPC isn’t just about sending data — it’s about sending data , , and .Whether you’re building the next generation of cloud services, IoT platforms, or complex distributed systems, gRPC offers a powerful toolkit for creating communication layers that are as dynamic and responsive as the applications they support.I’ll be diving deep into and why Kubernetes isn’t always the best fit for gRPC — along with how to ! Stay tuned for the full blog.

item.title: Kubernetes Cluster Creation using Ansible & Terraform: User data Bastion
item.author: Manohar Shetty
item.content:encoded: Setting up a Kubernetes cluster manually can be time-consuming and error-prone, especially when dealing with multiple instances, SSH key management, and configuration updates. Automating this process with Bash and Ansible simplifies infrastructure provisioning, ensures consistency, and reduces deployment time.In this blog, we will walk through a comprehensive automation script that:✅ Creates a dedicated user for automation✅ Manages SSH keys and securely distributes them✅ Installs AWS CLI and Ansible for infrastructure management✅ Fetches instance IPs dynamically from AWS✅ Updates Ansible inventory files and configuration settingsBy the end of this guide, you will have a fully automated workflow to set up and manage your Kubernetes cluster effortlessly.You can see the implementation video hereThe script automates the following steps:Creates a new user (ansible-user)..Grants sudo privileges without a password.. Switches to the newly created user.2. Installs and .Generates an SSH key if it does not exist.. Uploads the public key to an S3 bucket.. Fetches and configures the SSH key from S3.4. Ansible Playbook Cloning. Clones the ansible_playbook_k8s-installation repository.5. Updating Ansible Inventory. Dynamically fetches AWS EC2 instance IPs (for NFS, Bastion, Master, and Worker nodes).. Updates the inventory file (inventory.ini) accordingly.. Introduces delays to ensure instances are up before fetching IPs.6. Configuring Kubernetes Setup Files1. Updates and in main.yaml.2. Updates in nfs-setup/defaults/main.yaml.This ensures administrative privileges for executing the script.user_name="ansible-user"user_home="/home/$user_name"user_ssh_dir="$user_home/.ssh"3. Check if the User ExistsThis prevents duplicate user creation.if id "$user_name" &>/dev/null; then echo "User $user_name already exists."fiCreates ansible-user without a password.sudo adduser --disabled-password --gecos "" "$user_name"echo "User $user_name is created successfully"Allows the user to run commands without a password.echo "ansible-user ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL" | sudo tee /etc/sudoers.d/ansible-user7. Install AWS CLI and Ansiblesudo apt update -ysudo apt-get install -y awsclisudo apt-add-repository ppa:ansible/ansible -ysudo apt update -ysudo apt install ansible -ymkdir -p $user_ssh_dirchmod 700 $user_ssh_dirif [ ! -f "$user_ssh_dir/id_rsa" ]; then ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096 -f $user_ssh_dir/id_rsa -N ""chown -R $user_name:$user_name $user_home9. Upload SSH Key to AWS S3aws s3 cp $user_ssh_dir/id_rsa.pub s3://my-key/server.pub10. Download and Apply SSH Keyaws s3 cp s3://my-key/server.pub $user_ssh_dir/authorized_keyschmod 600 $user_ssh_dir/authorized_keyschown -R $user_name:$user_name $user_home11. Clone the Ansible Playbook Repositorygit clone "https://github.com/Manohar-1305/ansible_playbook_k8s-installation.git"12. Define Inventory File and LoggingINVENTORY_FILE="ansible_playbook_k8s-installation/ansible/inventories/inventory.ini"LOG_FILE="ansible_script.log"export AWS_REGION=ap-south-1log() { local message="$1" echo "$(date +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") - $message" | sudo tee -a "$LOG_FILE"}14. Update Inventory Entriesupdate_entry() { local section=$1 local ip=$3 log "Updating entry: Section: $section, Host: $host, IP: $ip" if ! grep -q "^\[$section\]" "$INVENTORY_FILE"; then log "Section $section not found. Adding section header." sudo bash -c "echo -e '\n[$section]' >>'$INVENTORY_FILE'" fi sudo sed -i "/^\[$section\]/,/^\[.*\]/{/^$host ansible_host=.*/d}" "$INVENTORY_FILE" sudo sed -i "/^\[$section\]/a $host ansible_host=$ip" "$INVENTORY_FILE"15. Fetch and Update NFS and Bastion IPsNFS_IP=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region "$AWS_REGION" --filters "Name=tag:Name,Values=nfs" --query "Reservations[*].Instances[*].PrivateIpAddress" --output text)BASTION_IP=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region "$AWS_REGION" --filters "Name=tag:Name,Values=bastion" --query "Reservations[*].Instances[*].PublicIpAddress" --output text)update_entry "local" "bastion" "$BASTION_IP"update_entry "nfs-server" "nfs" "$NFS_IP"16. Fetch and Update Master and Worker Node IPsmaster=("master1" "master2" "master3")worker=("worker1" "worker2" "worker3")for master_node in "${master[@]}"; do ip=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region "$AWS_REGION" --filters "Name=tag:Name,Values=$master_node" --query "Reservations[*].Instances[*].PrivateIpAddress" --output text) if [ -n "$ip" ]; then master_ips["$master_node"]=$ip fifor worker_node in "${worker[@]}"; do ip=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region "$AWS_REGION" --filters "Name=tag:Name,Values=$worker_node" --query "Reservations[*].Instances[*].PrivateIpAddress" --output text) if [ -n "$ip" ]; then worker_ips["$worker_node"]=$ip fi17. Update Inventory with Master and Worker Nodesupdate_entry "controlplane" "master3" "${master_ips[master3]}"update_entry "controlplane" "master2" "${master_ips[master2]}"update_entry "controlplane" "master1" "${master_ips[master1]}"update_entry "node" "worker3" "${worker_ips[worker3]}"update_entry "node" "worker2" "${worker_ips[worker2]}"update_entry "node" "worker1" "${worker_ips[worker1]}"18. Update Load Balancer and Advertise AddressLOAD_BALANCER_IP=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region "$AWS_REGION" --filters "Name=tag:Name,Values=master1" --query "Reservations[*].Instances[*].PrivateIpAddress" --output text)advertise_address=$LOAD_BALANCER_IPFILE_PATH="/home/ansible-user/ansible_playbook_k8s-installation/ansible/roles/first-master/defaults/main.yaml"if [ -f "$FILE_PATH" ]; then sudo sed -i.bak "s/^LOAD_BALANCER_IP:.*/LOAD_BALANCER_IP: ${LOAD_BALANCER_IP}/" "$FILE_PATH" sudo sed -i.bak "s/^advertise_address:.*/advertise_address: ${advertise_address}/" "$FILE_PATH"fi19. Update NFS IP in ConfigurationNFS_IP=$(aws ec2 describe-instances --region "$AWS_REGION" --filters "Name=tag:Name,Values=nfs" --query "Reservations[*].Instances[*].PrivateIpAddress" --output text)YAML_FILE="/home/ansible-user/ansible_playbook_k8s-installation/ansible/roles/nfs-setup/defaults/main.yaml"if [[ -n "$NFS_IP" ]]; then sudo sed -i "s/^nfs_ip:.*/nfs_ip: \"$NFS_IP\"/" "$YAML_FILE" echo "Updated nfs_ip in $YAML_FILE to $NFS_IP"else echo "Failed to fetch NFS IP. Please check the AWS command or instance state." exit 1This script automates Kubernetes infrastructure setup by creating a user, managing SSH keys, installing necessary tools, fetching AWS instance IPs, and updating Ansible inventory files. It significantly reduces manual intervention and ensures a consistent deployment process.

item.title: Data Pipeline Observability: Monitoring, Logging, and Alerting using OpenTelemetry, Prometheus…
item.author: Yousef Yousefi
item.content:encoded: Data Pipeline Observability: Monitoring, Logging, and Alerting using OpenTelemetry, Prometheus, Grafana, and Great ExpectationsIn modern , pipelines are no longer just about moving data — they must be observable, resilient, and self-healing. Without proper observability, data teams are blind to failures, latency spikes, and data quality issues.This article will cover advanced techniques for monitoring and debugging data pipelines, including:✅ Real-time monitoring with OpenTelemetry & Prometheus✅ Automated data quality checks using Great Expectations✅ Distributed logging for big data workloads✅ Anomaly detection and alerting with Grafana & PagerDutyBy the end, you’ll have a production-ready observability stack for ensuring across your data infrastructure.Why Observability Matters in Data PipelinesA well-monitored data pipeline can:✅ Detect failures before they impact end users✅ Track data quality anomalies (missing or corrupt data)✅ Alert teams in real time about performance degradation✅ Provide historical logs for root cause analysisKey Components of Data Observability Collect real-time metrics (latency, throughput, success/failure rates). Capture structured logs for debugging job failures. Notify teams when data issues arise (e.g., missing data). Track end-to-end execution of pipeline tasks.Implementing Advanced Monitoring in Data Pipelines1️⃣ Real-Time Monitoring with Prometheus & OpenTelemetryTo monitor data pipelines, we need real-time metrics collection. is the for instrumenting pipelines, and is used for storing & querying these metrics.Instrument a Spark Job with OpenTelemetry Metricsfrom opentelemetry import metricsfrom opentelemetry.sdk.metrics import MeterProviderfrom opentelemetry.sdk.metrics.export import PrometheusMetricReaderfrom prometheus_client import start_http_server# Start Prometheus exporterreader = PrometheusMetricReader()metrics.set_meter_provider(MeterProvider(metric_readers=[reader]))meter = metrics.get_meter("spark-job")records_processed = meter.create_counter( description="Number of records processed in Spark",# Start Prometheus endpointstart_http_server(8000)# Simulating metric updatesdef process_data(batch): records_processed.add(len(batch))This exposes real-time metrics to Prometheus.2️⃣ Configuring Prometheus to Collect Pipeline MetricsAdd the following to :filebeat.inputs: - type: log - "/var/log/spark-pipeline.log" hosts: ["http://elasticsearch:9200"]This makes Spark’s metrics available for monitoring.3️⃣ Distributed Logging for Data PipelinesLogs are critical for in distributed pipelines. Instead of using raw log files, a structured logging framework like ELK (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) helps aggregate logs across systems.Enable Structured Logging in Sparkfrom pyspark.sql import SparkSessionimport loggingspark = SparkSession.builder.appName("ObservabilityDemo").getOrCreate()# Configure structured logginglogger = logging.getLogger("spark-pipeline")logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)logger.info("Starting Spark job...")df = spark.read.json("s3://data-source/")logger.info(f"Read {df.count()} records from S3.")df.write.parquet("s3://processed-data/")logger.info("Data processing completed successfully.")This logs structured messages that can be collected by Elasticsearch.4️⃣ Streaming Logs to Elasticsearch using Filebeat1. Install to forward logs to Elasticsearch:sudo apt install filebeat2. Configure :filebeat.inputs: - type: log - "/var/log/spark-pipeline.log" hosts: ["http://elasticsearch:9200"]Now, all Spark logs are searchable in Kibana. Automating Data Quality Checks with Great ExpectationsBad data can cause silent failures. ensures that data meets predefined quality constraints before it moves downstream.Install Great Expectationspip install great_expectationsDefine Data Quality Constraintsimport great_expectations as gxcontext = gx.get_context()# Create expectation suitesuite = context.create_expectation_suite("data_quality_checks")batch = context.get_batch("s3://processed-data/")batch.expect_column_values_to_not_be_null("user_id")batch.expect_column_values_to_be_between("transaction_amount", min_value=1, max_value=10000)results = batch.validate()if not results["success"]: raise ValueError("Data quality check failed!")Now, data pipelines will fail fast if data doesn’t meet quality standards.6️⃣ Setting Up Alerts for Failures & AnomaliesSend Alerts to PagerDuty When Data Fails Validationimport requestsPAGERDUTY_API_KEY = "your_api_key"def send_alert(error_message): payload = { "routing_key": PAGERDUTY_API_KEY, "event_action": "trigger", "summary": error_message, "source": "data-pipeline" } requests.post("https://events.pagerduty.com/v2/enqueue", json=payload)Now, if a data pipeline fails, PagerDuty will alert the team.7️⃣ Visualizing Pipeline Health in GrafanaGrafana is used to visualize real-time metrics from Prometheus.Configure Prometheus as a data source in Grafana.Create to track:This guide covered end-to-end observability for data pipelines, including:✅ Real-time monitoring with Prometheus & OpenTelemetry✅ Structured logging with Elasticsearch & Filebeat✅ Data quality validation using Great Expectations✅ Automated alerting with PagerDuty✅ Visualization with Grafana

item.title: 22 Python Scripts Every DevOps Engineer Should Automate
item.author: Obafemi

item.title: Mastering Dependency Injection in Spring Boot Microservices
item.author: Pradeep K
item.content:encoded: Dependency Injection (DI) is a fundamental concept in Spring Boot that enables better code modularity, testability, and maintainability. In a microservices architecture, mastering DI ensures efficient service communication, reduces coupling, and enhances scalability.What is Dependency Injection?Dependency Injection is a design pattern that allows a class to receive its dependencies from an external source rather than creating them itself. In Spring Boot, DI is managed using the Spring IoC (Inversion of Control) container, which injects dependencies at runtime.Types of Dependency Injection in Spring BootSpring Boot supports three types of Dependency Injection: (Preferred method)Constructor injection is recommended as it ensures and are always initialized.@Servicepublic class OrderService { private final PaymentService paymentService; public OrderService(PaymentService paymentService) { this.paymentService = paymentService; } public void processOrder() { paymentService.processPayment();}Field injection is simpler but as it makes unit testing harder.@Servicepublic class OrderService { private PaymentService paymentService; public void processOrder() { paymentService.processPayment();}Setter injection allows optional dependencies and flexibility but can lead to inconsistent state if not handled properly.@Servicepublic class OrderService { private PaymentService paymentService; public void setPaymentService(PaymentService paymentService) { this.paymentService = paymentService; } public void processOrder() { paymentService.processPayment();}Using @Component, @Service, and @RepositorySpring Boot uses annotations to automatically manage dependencies: — Generic component for DI. — Used for service layer logic. — Used for data access layer, integrating with databases.@Componentpublic class NotificationService { public void sendNotification() { System.out.println("Sending notification...");}Configuring Dependency Injection with @BeanFor more control, you can define beans in a configuration class:@Configurationpublic class AppConfig { public PaymentService paymentService() { return new PaymentService(); }Managing Dependencies in MicroservicesIn a microservices architecture, services communicate using REST, messaging, or service discovery. Dependency injection helps manage inter-service dependencies efficiently.Example: Injecting a Feign Client for Microservices CommunicationSpring Cloud Feign simplifies service-to-service communication.@FeignClient(name = "payment-service")public interface PaymentClient { @GetMapping("/payment/process") String processPayment();Injecting the Feign client:@Servicepublic class OrderService { private final PaymentClient paymentClient; public OrderService(PaymentClient paymentClient) { this.paymentClient = paymentClient; } public void processOrder() { System.out.println(paymentClient.processPayment());}Mastering Dependency Injection in Spring Boot microservices ensures efficient code structure, reduces coupling, and enhances maintainability. By leveraging constructor injection, @Component-based beans, and externalized configurations, you can build scalable and modular microservices with ease.Would you like to explore advanced DI techniques like Spring Profiles, Qualifiers, or Custom Bean Factories

item.content:encoded: Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe in London from April 1 - 4, 2025. Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io
